source_alignment.subject: Science
source_alignment.topic: Living things and their habitats: classification
source_alignment.source_statement: Describe how living things are classified into broad groups according to common observable characteristics and based on similarities and differences, including micro-organisms, plants and animals; give reasons for classifying plants and animals based on specific characteristics.
source_alignment.derived_objective_note: This Year 6 pack turns classification into an evidence-rich decision process: learners use observable features, build branching keys, justify group choices and avoid over-reliance on one superficial trait.

objective.child_goal: I can classify living things using observable evidence and explain my rule.
objective.statement: Classify animals, plants and micro-organisms into broad groups using similarities, differences and specific observable characteristics.
misconceptions: Groups living things by where they are found rather than by shared observable characteristics.
Uses one visible feature, such as colour or size, even when it does not separate the organisms scientifically.
Thinks all micro-organisms are harmful or visible to the naked eye.
Confuses a branching key question with a guess about the organism's name.
Treats a classification group as fixed without checking new evidence.

vocabulary.child_explanation: Sorting living things into groups using shared characteristics.
vocabulary.audio_script: Classification means grouping living things using evidence about their shared characteristics.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: Organism cards moving into labelled evidence groups.

vocabulary.child_explanation: An observable feature that can help describe or classify a living thing.
vocabulary.audio_script: A characteristic is a feature you can observe or describe carefully.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: Magnifier highlighting leaf shape, backbone, segments and spores.

vocabulary.child_explanation: A set of yes-or-no questions that helps identify or classify living things.
vocabulary.audio_script: A branching key uses clear yes-or-no questions to guide a classification decision.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: Two-path key with large yes and no route buttons.

vocabulary.child_explanation: A living thing that is usually too small to see without a microscope.
vocabulary.audio_script: A micro-organism is usually too small to see without a microscope. Some are helpful, some are harmful, and many are simply part of ecosystems.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: Microscope icon beside yeast, bacteria and mould model cards.

vocabulary.child_explanation: Information from observation that supports a classification decision.
vocabulary.audio_script: Evidence is what you observed that supports your classification.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: Observation notes linked to a group label.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Choose only features you can observe or describe from the organism cards.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Separate evidence-based features from guesses, habitat labels and personal preferences.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: Scientists classify living things using evidence. Start with features you can observe or describe clearly.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Organism cards with feature chips such as backbone, wings, leaf veins, spores and body segments.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Follow the yes-or-no questions to classify the mystery organism.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Introduce a branching key as a structured route through observable evidence.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: A good branching key question splits the evidence clearly. It should not ask you to guess the name too soon.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Large yes/no branching route with one highlighted question at a time.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Use evidence to decide whether this animal belongs with vertebrates or invertebrates.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Model reasoned classification using a specific feature rather than appearance alone.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: We are not sorting by cute, scary or where it lives. We are checking whether the evidence shows a backbone.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: X-ray style backbone evidence card compared with a body-segment evidence card.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Build two clear questions that separate these four living things.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Practise designing classification questions that split groups fairly and visibly.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: Try a question that each organism can answer yes or no. If all cards go the same way, the question does not help yet.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Four organism cards route through two editable yes/no question nodes.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Which statement is careful and scientific about micro-organisms?
teaching_sequence.audio_script: Micro-organisms are living things that are usually too small to see without a microscope. They are not all the same.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Helpful yeast, decomposer mould and harmful bacteria cards with caution labels.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Teach Nova why 'it lives in water' may not be enough evidence for a scientific group.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Consolidate classification reasoning and evidence quality.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: I sorted these together because they all live in water. What evidence should I check next?
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Companion shows fish, duckweed and pond snail; feature evidence overlays appear after feedback.

manipulatives.purpose: Guide learners through yes/no classification decisions using observable evidence cards.
manipulatives.interaction_notes: Learners inspect organism cards, choose yes/no routes, replay vocabulary and justify the final group with selected evidence.
manipulatives.accessibility_notes: Each branch has text, symbol and audio labels; switch access scans yes/no choices; reduced-motion mode shows route breadcrumbs instead of animated branching.

manipulatives.purpose: Let learners construct and test classification questions against a small organism set.
manipulatives.interaction_notes: Learners write or select observable yes/no questions, route cards, test whether groups split usefully and revise weak questions.
manipulatives.accessibility_notes: Provides direct-select question banks, large card targets, keyboard movement, spoken reread and a simple-text mode.

manipulatives.purpose: Sort organism evidence by shared characteristics before building a key.
manipulatives.interaction_notes: Learners group cards by features, mark the evidence used and reject unsafe or non-scientific sort rules.
manipulatives.accessibility_notes: Cards include alt text, texture icons, non-colour cues and optional two-card comparison mode.

practice.interleaving_notes: Interleave feature evidence, branching key navigation, key construction and micro-organism claims. Revisit Year 4 habitat grouping so learners distinguish habitat from classification evidence.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Classify organisms using observable features such as backbone, leaf structure, body segments, spores and flowering.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: organism card set + candidate feature rules + evidence chips + group label
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Avoid culturally narrow organism sets; use non-colour evidence and accurate but age-appropriate features.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Follow yes/no questions to identify or classify mystery organisms.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: small key + mystery organism evidence + route choices + final group
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Questions must be answerable from evidence shown and should not rely on specialist taxonomy beyond Year 6.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Build useful yes/no questions that split organism sets and can be tested.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: four to six organism cards + question bank + route preview + revise prompt
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Include examples where a tempting question sends all cards the same way.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Use careful claims about micro-organisms, including helpful, harmful and decomposer examples.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: micro-organism scenario + claim choices + evidence cards + because explanation
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Safeguarding review must keep illness examples calm, factual and non-alarming.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Maintain key vocabulary and evidence-based grouping through short spaced retrieval.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: single feature decision or two-step key route + immediate explanation
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Keep retrieval low text and vary animal, plant and micro-organism examples.

body.prompt: Use the key route for the pond snail. Does the evidence show a backbone?

hints: Use the evidence shown, not the habitat.
A snail is an invertebrate because it does not have a backbone.
question_variants.explanation: The key asks about a backbone. The pond snail evidence shows no backbone, so the route goes to invertebrate evidence.

body.prompt: Which question best separates an oak tree, fern, mushroom and yeast?
choices: Is it green in the picture?

hints: A useful key question is clear and evidence-based.
Opinions and picture colour are not reliable classification questions.
question_variants.explanation: Can it make seeds? is an observable biological question that can separate some plant and fungus examples more usefully than opinions or picture colour.

body.prompt: Sort the evidence card 'has a backbone' into the best classification use.
choices: Useful for separating vertebrates and invertebrates
Not evidence because it is inside the body

expected_answer.value: Useful for separating vertebrates and invertebrates

hints: Some features are internal but still observable with evidence.
Backbone evidence is used for vertebrate classification.
question_variants.explanation: Backbone evidence is a specific characteristic used to separate vertebrates from invertebrates.

body.prompt: Which claim is most careful about micro-organisms?
choices: Micro-organisms are usually too small to see without a microscope, and they can be helpful, harmful or neutral.
A micro-organism is any tiny speck of dust.

expected_answer.value: Micro-organisms are usually too small to see without a microscope, and they can be helpful, harmful or neutral.

hints: Do not overgeneralise: they are not all harmful.
question_variants.explanation: The careful claim names scale and avoids saying all micro-organisms have the same effect.

body.prompt: Nova grouped a fish, duckweed and pond snail because they all live in water. What should Nova check next?
choices: Observable characteristics such as backbone, plant structures or shell/body evidence
Which one is the brightest colour
Which one the learner likes most

expected_answer.value: Observable characteristics such as backbone, plant structures or shell/body evidence

hints: Habitat can be useful context, but classification needs characteristics.
Look for evidence that separates living things by features.
question_variants.explanation: Living in water does not make these living things the same biological group. Nova should check specific observable characteristics.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Groups living things by where they are found rather than by shared observable characteristics.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: Learner groups fish, plant and invertebrate examples together only because they share a habitat.
repair_sequence: Keep habitat as a context card.
Re-sort using a biological characteristic and explain the rule.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: Learner uses a characteristic such as backbone, flowering or body segments to justify a group.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Uses one visible feature even when it does not separate organisms scientifically.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: Learner chooses colour, size or picture style over a relevant biological feature.
repair_sequence: Test whether the chosen feature stays reliable across examples.
Compare with a scientific feature card.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: Learner rejects an unreliable feature and selects a specific observable biological characteristic.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Thinks all micro-organisms are harmful or visible to the naked eye.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: Learner chooses absolute claims about all micro-organisms.
repair_sequence: Sort helpful, harmful and decomposer examples.
Build a careful claim using can, usually and some.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: Learner states a qualified claim about micro-organisms without alarmist overgeneralisation.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Confuses a branching key question with a guess about the organism's name.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: Learner tries to identify a name before answering the evidence question.
repair_sequence: Read the current yes/no question aloud.
Point to the evidence card that answers it.
Follow the route before naming the organism or group.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: Learner completes a new key route by answering questions in order.

adaptive_support.low_sensory: Use calm field-guide cards, no surprise reveals and non-alarming micro-organism examples.
adaptive_support.reduced_motion: Show static key breadcrumbs and card-group snapshots instead of animated branching.
adaptive_support.audio_first: Read organism names, evidence chips and yes/no questions with replay controls.
adaptive_support.reading_support: Use simple-text feature chips, labelled icons and picture-supported vocabulary cards.
adaptive_support.attention_support: Expose one key question at a time and hide unused branches until needed.
adaptive_support.processing_support: Allow side-by-side comparison of two organisms before the full sort.
adaptive_support.confidence_support: Frame key-building as test-and-revise so learners can improve rules without feeling wrong.
adaptive_support.eal_support: Preteach classify, characteristic, organism, micro-organism, evidence and key with visual glossary cards.

evidence.mastery_rule: Expected requires using observable characteristics to follow a key and justify a classification; secure requires building or revising a key question and making careful micro-organism claims.
evidence.parent_summary: Can classify living things using observable evidence and explain why the chosen grouping is scientifically useful.
evidence.teacher_evidence: Uses evidence-based characteristics, follows and builds branching keys, avoids habitat-only grouping and qualifies claims about micro-organisms.
next_actions: Interleave classification with Year 6 evolution evidence.
Use working-scientifically language to evaluate classification rules.
Bridge to Year 7 cells and ecosystems classification vocabulary.

qa.notes: Review-status Year 6 classification sample. Science and SEND review should check organism examples, micro-organism language, non-colour evidence and branching-key accessibility before pilot.
